llama3.1-8b.text:
Here is the translated paragraph:

The Brabançon clan's taunts covered the insulting Burgundian charivari at least twenty times. This made a blood-soaked tournament; the assault was fierce; they fought body to body. They attacked with the maul or sickle, with harpoon or pilum. It lasted all day. Then, using a subtle calculation, the bold Brabançon champion captured his opponent: thus the Archduke was beaten, thoroughly beaten. The peace was concluded. We danced in all cabbalistic gatherings to the sound of the hautbois, biniou, and drum. We acclaimed the imaginative paladin with great cries. He was knighted. He was made Grand Admiral. He went to the Palace to see Sibylla. Sibylla pleased him. He troubled Sibylla.

Oh, you who read this, we must no longer fail to free you, although, as sure as anything, you understood straight away that Sturmi carried on his back: yes, you were right, it was Aignan. For Aignan did not know, like Oidipos, that Sibylla was his mother. And Sibylla did not know that Aignan was her son. And Sibylla conceived a mad love for Aignan. And Aignan conceived a mad love for Sibylla. And Aignan knew Sibylla. And Sibylla knew Aignan.

A cursed chance announced to the lovers their kinship, uniting Aignan and Sibylla. Sibylla made an oration, built a hospital where we washed the asphyxiating panard of the vagabond, where we treated him for nothing. Aignan dressed in rags from a tramp, in a coarse rope he wore as mortification; he took a stick, but no sack, nor quarter-aluminium. He left, one evening, a palace 47 48 where he had known peace. He departed far away. He wanted to humiliate himself. He wanted to suffer the condemnation of the Almighty.

He slept the night in a wood. He was hungry. He arrived, after a hard journey which took him at least three days, on the edge of a lake. He knocked on the door of a peasant's house. They opened it for him: –Is there hereabouts, he wanted to know, a Locus Solus where I could punish my Unbelievable Forfeit forever?

–There is, said the obtuse peasant, at the midpoint of the lac, an island, no, rather a rock, a peak, abrupt as desired, where you can wallow in your damnable grief!

–Will you take me there? he implored Aignan.

–Very well, he said, surprised, but you will rot there until the End. –As He has always willed it, he psalmodized Aignan.

–So be it, they accompanied him. They took him there, to the Island of the Great Pardon. They tied him to the neck of a halter, or rather a garrot. Aignan devoted himself to contrition. A nourishing humus which exuded from a hole in the rock became his daily bread forever. He was subjected to hurricanes, gales, icy mistral, scorching khamsin, whirling sirocco. He was subjected to razes, typhoons...

llama3.1-8b.text:
Here is the translated paragraph:

hillside rotted like a dried-up tinder. He was bare.
He felt cold, he felt hot; he froze, he roasted.

Then, undernourished, poorly nourished, despite
the kindly humus that God gave him in his compassion,
he finally began to thin: he thinned, he continued
to thin. He became a wretch. He insisted on
thinning. He thinned until he diminished, until
he shortened. He shrank; first he was less tall than
a dwarf, then, at last, a true homunculus, a diminutive,
a human not bigger than an urchin...

But it came to pass, eighteen years later, that
Paul Six of the time was quite dying. At the Vatican
there was a nice commotion: one had to guarantee
the paternity, choose a successor. They held at least eight
scrutines: here they named a fool, there a puffy man;
here a schizoid, there a madman. In the Consistorial Palace,
corruption ran wild: they offered pontificate for a million.
Things were going badly. Faith wandered about.
No one adored his Saint Patron anymore.

Then a divine anger obscured the sky. Then,
one day, He visited a Cardinal; He appeared as
a bloody sheep; a bed of aromatic golden buttons accompanied Him.

—Oh, Cardinal, said His voice, listen to me: you have
a Pope. I made my choice. He is called Aignan.
We chose him because he was rotting eighteen years ago on
a rock battered by My tide.

—Oh, divine sheep, Oh Almighty, stuttered the adoring Cardinal,
let it be done according to Your good will!

They went about asking everywhere about an Aignan
rotting on a rock. They finally managed, not without difficulty,
to arrive at the lake shore; they knocked on the door of
the peasant's house who, eighteen years earlier, had led Aignan onto the island.
But first, he was obstinate:
—Aignan doesn't know me, he said. Island doesn't know me.
There is no island around here.

Then the bait of gain helped; they finally found out everything:
they navigated to the island; they climbed up there
not without great trouble. But, there above, to the great dismay
of the Cardinal whose faith wavered then, despite
the affirmation of the Divine Sheep, there was no Aignan.
He had completely vanished, proving that he did not know
the compassion of the Almighty...

Despite Thomas Mann, my conclusion imposed itself,
confided Anton Voyl when he put a final point on his novel;
to his draft rather, if you will, or else to his synopsis,
since he imagined his narration in abundance, but never arrived
at the crucial moment of the Discourse: his proposal only came to twenty-five or twenty-six notes:
he embroidered over five or six points; he made a fairly fine portrait of Aignan;
he sketched an Archduke quite striking (« a great scoundrel,
smooth-shaven, with long reddish sideburns »: one sees
that he was inspired by the doctor who had indeed flattered him);
he fussed over it, but only for a brief instant.

The amusing patois of that crafty peasant Finaud who led Aignan to his island (« Fouchtra for Catarina! Boudui! There's a young buck who'll go fresher than ever to tend his paturin at the bakery, Jarnicot! »); he gave us a shortcut from a burin so subtle that Paul Morand, Giraudoux or Maupassant could have offered it to their public without blushing. But he didn't push further: in his journal, he justified himself with an unheard-of baralipton: if, he postulated, my novel could be accomplished, we must accomplish it; but, he pursued, if it were accomplished, wouldn't it open up a knowledge so clear, so pure, so harsh that none of us would survive its instant? For, he pursued, fiction always wanted there to be only one Aignan to free himself from the Sphinx. Aignan vanished, no triumphant Logos will ever offer his consoling power again. Thus, he concluded, no discourse will ever abolish chance. Yet, he added lower down, we have no choice: we must know at all costs that a Sphinx could attack us at any instant; we must know, have we ever known, that at any instant it would be enough to say a word, a sound, an affirmation, a negation, and we'd have vanquished it immediately. For—thus said Zarathoustra—no Sphinx has ever made its nest outside the Human Palace... Where, notwithstanding a certain • Vol du Bourdon>,

Anton Voyl vanished on All Saints' Day.
Three days earlier, he had read in an evening newspaper a report that alarmed him greatly:
An individual whose power was so darkly feared that his anonymity was protected, sneaking into a local office of the Chief Commissariat at night, stole a vital letter said to reveal the compromising involvement of the trio of constables commanding the Poulaga House. We had to retrieve the unwanted manuscript as soon as possible, or else the brazen thief would know who to offer it to. But although we were sure he'd hidden it in his house, which we searched at least twenty times, we couldn't find it.
Taking a long shot, a Commander, Romain Didot, accompanied by his trusted aide Garamond, went to see Dupin, whose infallible intuition was praised.
—A priori, he told him, we wouldn't have suffered from the theft so greatly; for any ordinary letter, if they'd stolen an x or a y from us, it would have been a minor setback. But here, this pilfering involves a bourdon too important...
—A bourdon? Dupin was intrigued, who, to be sure, didn't know the meaning of the word.
—I apologize for using jargon, smiled Didot: let's say that we think it concerns a vital theft which abolishes, makes vain, makes obsolete all concern with organization: it weakens our powers in the

—Or therefore, wished to know Dupin, the house of the scoundrel had been subjected twenty times to inquisition?
—Yes, admitted Didot, but we made blank shot always. We rummaged everywhere.
—Here's what appears very clear to me, affirmed Dupin: you probe everywhere, you scan walls or ceilings, but without any result, for you have a crystalline object but can't see it: haven't you understood, big fool, that surely your fellow had chosen a more subtle shelter: namely, he hadn't hidden his loot, he had merely sullied or withered it like one makes of an ordinary word, then balled up in an undersea vessel where you took him at least ten times, sans, knowing, without wanting to know if it was not a trivial rag, but the primary fold!
—But, objected Didot, there would be no hand-to-hand combat!
—Come on, jeered Dupin.
He put on his mackintosh, took his riflescope, went out, affirming:
—I'm going. In an instant, you'll have your document.
Though he had reason, at least in his calculation, he missed the mark.
—Once, I had a good draught, murmured he.
Then, for consolation, he occupied himself, leaving the PJ to its worries, with an orangutan who had committed three murders.
If Dupin didn't know, though he understood it all by instinct from A to Z, there would be no forgiveness for me, noted Anton Voyl in his Journal.
He put a note to the friends he had. In it he said:
—I would have slept soundly all my life. I would have given myself a good kip. But he vanished! Who? How? Never mind! It's gone. Now it's my turn, today, I'll go till death, till the great white forgetfulness, till omission. A must Pardon. I would have liked to know. A torturing illness twisted me. My voice is like a stammering whisper. O my death, be the ransom of the mad transport that inhabited me. Anton Voyl ».
There was a postscript, an astonishing postscript, showing that Anton Voyl had lost his mind: « Let's take ten good whiskies to the cheating lawyer who smoked at the zoo •>.
Finally, paraphrasing, there were three horizontal dashes (one of which seemed shorter) crossed out by a confused scrawl.
Did he kill himself? Did he put a gun to his zygoma? Did he open up in a warm bath? Did he drink aqua-toffana? Did he throw his car into an unfathomable, bottomless whirlpool without end till the evening of the Great Day, till the day of the Great Evening? Did he turn on the gas? Did he do harakiri? Did he get doused with napalm? Did he fall from a bridge into a dark tide that absorbed him?
Nobody ever knew if he chose his own death,

llama3.1-8b.text:
Aval Ivan by fish, at Milan Odilon succumbed who attended Lucchino Visconti an os too pointed inserted in his pharynx. In Honolulu Urbain died of hirudination: a colossal lombric sucked all his blood, they did but too late twenty transfusions. Amaury had no longer than one son surviving Yvon; however he loved less Yvon because Yvan living far away saw his dad three times in year never more. Amaury Conson rummaged at bottom the villa of Anton Voyl. He saw with neighbor who told him the ablation of sinus. He asked everywhere where he could. Anton Voyl lived in a dim room without any apparatus, without any attraction, without care for standing: walls whitened by lime, dirty carpets made from bad cotton which fell apart in flakes. There was a cramped salon, living-room at abandonment where a damp sofa showed its hair joined a stinking cupboard reeking oignon. A band-aid fixed three horror chromos to battants of a creaking wardrobe. The bow-window with opalin vitrail gave a grey day, pale. There was for bed a monastic châlit, a bad pallet with drooping cushions, with not delicious sheets. He had no lavabo but a dark recess, a jug, a pot, a bowl, a razor, a towel where a mole made his lunch.

Amaury opened, one by one, a pile of in-octavos dirty backs, bent plates which accumulated on three creaking shelves. Each one carried a whole heap of annotations, marginalia which he ran but which he understood very badly. He however distinguished five or six books which Anton Voyl seemed to have submitted to more profound work: Art and Illusion by Gombrich, Cosmos by Witold Gombrowicz, The Opoponax by Monica Wittig, Doktor Faustus by Thomas Mann, Noam Chomsky, Roman Jakobson, White or Forgetfulness of Aragn. Then Amaury laid hand on a strong cardboard which he opened. He found many manuscripts proving Anton had taste for instruction because he kept with meticulous care the acquired that one inculcated him long ago. Thus, reading word by word, Amaury could run over instructive curriculum studiorum of Anton.

There was first some French:

Where we lived formerly there were neither autos, nor taxis, nor buses; we went sometimes, my cousin accompanied me, see Linda who lived in a canton 1•oisin. But not having auto, it us was necessary to run all along course; otherwise we arrived too late: Linda had disappeared. One day came however Os Linda left for always. Our parents would have banished her forever; but there behold we loved. We loved so her perfume, her radiant air, her blouson, her brown trousers too
